title: Redstone Basics
description: Learn the fundamentals of Minecraft redstone engineering — from torches to logic gates!
author: CraftMind

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Redstone Torches
lessons.description: Discover the power source that started it all — the humble redstone torch!
objectives: Place a redstone torch to power a block

steps.description: Walk to the redstone lab (follow the torches!)
Look at the demo setup — a redstone torch powering a lamp
Place a redstone torch on the marked block to power the lamp next to it
Talk to ProfBlock to explain what you observed about the torch signal
Power all 3 lamps using only 2 redstone torches — find the right positions!
Discovery Zone: Figure out how to make a redstone torch toggle — power it and unpower it rapidly using a lever!

discovery.title: The Torch Toggle Mystery
discovery.description: You have a lever and a redstone torch. Figure out: what happens if you flick the lever on and off really fast? Try it!
discovery.solution: The torch burns out and turns into a regular item. This is a real mechanic in Minecraft!
hints: Try placing a redstone torch on a block, then power that block with a lever.
What happens when you flip the lever on? What about off?
Try flipping it on and off rapidly — like, really fast. Something unexpected might happen!
discovery.realWorldInsight: Real electronic components can burn out too! Overvoltage or rapid switching can damage transistors and capacitors, just like the redstone torch burns out.

quiz.question: What does a redstone torch power?
options: Only the block it's placed on
All adjacent blocks and redstone dust
quiz.answer: All adjacent blocks and redstone dust
quiz.explanation: A redstone torch powers the block it's attached to and all adjacent blocks, plus any redstone dust connected to those blocks.

quiz.question: Can a redstone torch be placed on any side of a block?
options: On top and on the four sides, but not the bottom
quiz.answer: On top and on the four sides, but not the bottom
quiz.explanation: Redstone torches can be placed on top and the four vertical sides of most solid blocks, but not underneath.

quiz.question: What happens when a block already powered by redstone has a torch placed on it?
quiz.explanation: A redstone torch on a powered block will turn off (and eventually burn out if toggled rapidly). This is the key to NOT gates!
quiz.commonMistake: People often think a torch ON a powered block gets STRONGER — actually the opposite happens! The power turns it off.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Redstone Dust & Wiring
lessons.description: Learn how redstone dust carries signals — think of it as Minecraft's electrical wiring!
objectives: Place redstone dust to create a signal path
Understand signal strength decreases over distance
Connect a power source to a device

steps.description: Head to the wiring workshop at the next station
Check out the long redstone dust trail — notice how the lamp brightness changes with distance
Build your own redstone wire: place dust from the lever to the lamp, exactly 15 blocks long
Add a repeater at block 8 to extend the signal — the lamp at the end should now be fully lit!

quiz.question: How far can a redstone dust signal travel before it dies?
quiz.explanation: Redstone signals start at strength 15 and decrease by 1 for each block of dust. After 15 blocks, the signal reaches 0 and dies.

quiz.question: What item can extend a redstone signal beyond 15 blocks?
quiz.explanation: A redstone repeater refreshes a signal to full strength (15), allowing you to extend circuits indefinitely.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Repeaters & Comparators
lessons.description: Master the repeater — the workhorse of every redstone circuit!
objectives: Place and orient a redstone repeater
Use a repeater as a diode (one-way signal)

steps.description: Move to the repeater testing station
Flip the lever and watch the 4 repeaters — notice how each has a different delay
Create a circuit with 3 repeaters set to 2-tick delay each, then time how long the lamp takes to light
Build a signal that reaches the lamp in exactly 4 ticks using only repeaters (tip: 2+2, 1+3, etc.)

quiz.question: How many tick delay settings does a repeater have?
quiz.explanation: A repeater has 4 delay settings: 1, 2, 3, and 4 redstone ticks (each = 2 game ticks). Right-click to cycle through them.

quiz.question: True or false: a repeater can send a signal backwards.
quiz.explanation: Repeaters are diodes — they only pass signals in one direction (the direction they're facing). This is incredibly useful for preventing signal interference.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Pistons & Doors
lessons.description: Make things move! Build your first piston-powered redstone door!
objectives: Use redstone to trigger the door with a button

steps.description: Enter the piston workshop through... well, you'll build the entrance!
Watch the demo: a sticky piston pulls and pushes blocks when powered/unpowered
Place 2 pistons (1 sticky, 1 regular) as shown in the diagram on the wall
Wire up a button to both pistons — press it and your door should open!
Modify the circuit so the door closes automatically after 3 seconds (hint: use a repeater as a delay)

quiz.question: What's the difference between a piston and a sticky piston?
quiz.explanation: A sticky piston has slime on its face — when it retracts, it pulls the block in front of it back too. Regular pistons just push.

quiz.question: What happens if you power a piston while something is already in front of it?
quiz.explanation: The piston won't extend if there's a block (or entity) blocking it. Redstone only works if there's room for the mechanism to move!

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Basic Logic Gates
lessons.description: The building blocks of computing — AND, OR, and NOT gates, all in Minecraft!
objectives: Build a NOT gate using a redstone torch
Build an AND gate using 2 torches
Understand how logic gates combine to make decisions

steps.description: Enter the Logic Lab — welcome to computer science! 🖥️
Study the 3 demo gates (NOT, AND, OR) — flip their input levers and watch the output lamps
Build a NOT gate: place a block, put a torch on it, wire input to the block — torch output is inverted!
Build an AND gate: both input levers must be ON for the lamp to light. Use 2 torches and 1 dust line.
BONUS: Combine NOT + AND to make a NAND gate, then use it to open the exit door! Only 1 correct lever combo works!
Discovery Zone: Build an XOR gate using only torches and dust. XOR means 'one or the other, but not both'.

discovery.title: The XOR Challenge
discovery.description: Can you build a circuit where the lamp lights when EXACTLY ONE input is on? Not zero, not both — just one! This is an XOR gate.
discovery.solution: An XOR gate can be built by combining AND, OR, and NOT gates: (A OR B) AND NOT (A AND B). Or more simply in Minecraft, by connecting two torch outputs together.
hints: Think about what you've learned: AND (both), OR (any), NOT (opposite). Can you combine them?
XOR = (A OR B) AND NOT (A AND B). Try building each part separately first.

Here's a direct Minecraft approach: Place two blocks side by side. Put a torch on each. Wire input A to block A and input B to block B. The torch outputs feed into a single dust line that goes to the lamp.
discovery.realWorldInsight: XOR gates are fundamental to addition in computers! Your CPU adds numbers using XOR gates for the sum bits. You just built part of a calculator! 🧮

quiz.question: In a NOT gate, what happens when the input is ON (powered)?
quiz.explanation: A NOT gate inverts the signal. When you power the block a torch is on, the torch turns OFF — the output is the opposite of the input.
quiz.commonMistake: Students often expect the output to be the same as the input. Remember: redstone torches turn OFF when their block is powered — that's the inversion!

quiz.question: For an AND gate, how many inputs must be ON for the output to be ON?
quiz.explanation: An AND gate only outputs ON when ALL inputs are ON. It's like a security door that needs every lock to be unlocked.

quiz.question: What real-world device uses AND logic?
quiz.explanation: Many devices use AND logic! A microwave door AND start button must both be activated. A washing machine won't spin until the door is locked AND water is filled AND cycle is set.
